front: What is the fundamental principle of Depth-First Search (DFS)?
back: DFS is an uninformed graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It uses a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) stack, either explicitly or via recursion, to manage the nodes to be explored.

front: What are the key data structures used to implement DFS?
back: A stack (to manage the frontier nodes) and a set (or list) to keep track of visited nodes to prevent cycles and redundant processing.

front: What are the time and space complexity of DFS on a graph?
back: Time Complexity: O(|V| + |E|), where |V| is the number of vertices and |E| is the number of edges. Space Complexity: O(|V|) in the worst case, as the stack must store all nodes along a single path from the root to a leaf.

front: What is a major disadvantage of DFS for uninformed search?
back: DFS is not complete in infinite state spaces or finite spaces with cycles unless cycle detection (a visited set) is implemented. Even with cycle detection, it is not optimal; it may find a solution that is not the shortest path.

front: What is the difference between Tree-Search DFS and Graph-Search DFS?
back: Tree-Search DFS does not check for repeated states and can get stuck in infinite loops in graphs with cycles. Graph-Search DFS maintains a 'visited' set to avoid expanding the same state more than once, preventing infinite loops.
